whyItMatters: Components using the Default change detection strategy are checked on every change detection cycle, even when their inputs haven't changed. This causes unnecessary template re-evaluation and DOM diffing, degrading performance in large component trees.
howToFix: Add `changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush` to the component decorator
Ensure inputs are immutable objects or use observables with the async pipe
Use signals or OnPush-compatible patterns for state updates
Trigger manual change detection with `ChangeDetectorRef.markForCheck()` when needed

whyItMatters: Without a trackBy function, Angular destroys and recreates all DOM elements in an *ngFor list whenever the array reference changes. This causes expensive DOM operations and potential loss of element state like focus or scroll position.
howToFix: Add a `trackBy` function that returns a unique identifier for each item
Use a stable property like `id` as the tracking key
For the new @for syntax, add a `track` expression

codeExample.after:
{{user.name}}
trackById(index: number, user: User): number { return user.id; }

whyItMatters: Components with more than 800 DOM nodes create heavy layout and paint workloads. Each node participates in style calculation, layout, and compositing, causing frame drops and sluggish interactions especially on lower-end devices.
howToFix: Break the component into smaller child components with focused responsibilities
Use virtual scrolling (CDK VirtualScrollViewport) for long lists
Implement lazy rendering with *ngIf or @defer for off-screen content
Remove unnecessary wrapper elements and flatten the DOM structure
Use CSS-only solutions instead of DOM elements where possible

codeExample.before: {{item.name}}

whyItMatters: Layout thrashing occurs when code alternates between reading layout properties and writing styles, forcing the browser to recalculate layout multiple times per frame. This can turn a single frame into dozens of forced synchronous layouts, causing visible jank.
howToFix: Batch all DOM reads together before performing any writes
Use requestAnimationFrame to separate read and write phases
Cache layout values instead of reading them repeatedly
Consider using the Web Animations API or CSS transitions instead of JS-driven layout changes

whyItMatters: A rendering phase exceeding 16ms means the browser cannot maintain 60fps, causing dropped frames and visible stuttering. Users perceive this as lag during scrolling, animations, or interactions.
howToFix: Reduce the number of bindings evaluated per change detection cycle
Move expensive computations to memoized pipes or computed signals
Split large templates into smaller OnPush components
Defer non-critical rendering with @defer or requestIdleCallback

codeExample.after:
{{item | expensiveValue}}
@Pipe({ name: 'expensiveValue', pure: true }) export class ExpensiveValuePipe { transform(item: Item): string { ... } }

whyItMatters: Accessing layout-triggering properties (offsetHeight, getBoundingClientRect, etc.) after modifying styles forces the browser to perform a synchronous layout calculation. This blocks the main thread and can cascade into severe performance degradation.
howToFix: Read layout properties before making any style changes
Use ResizeObserver instead of polling layout properties
Batch style mutations using classList or cssText
Defer layout reads to the next frame with requestAnimationFrame

whyItMatters: Calling functions directly in templates causes them to execute on every change detection cycle. Even simple functions add up quickly in large component trees, leading to unnecessary CPU usage and slower rendering.
howToFix: Replace template function calls with pure pipes for transformations
Use computed signals for derived values
Pre-compute values in the component class and bind to properties
Use memoization if the function is unavoidable

codeExample.after: {{user | fullName}} @Pipe({ name: 'fullName', pure: true }) export class FullNamePipe { transform(user: User): string { return `${user.first} ${user.last}`; } }

whyItMatters: Lists rendered without tracking expressions force Angular to destroy and recreate DOM nodes on every data change. This wastes browser resources on DOM allocation and garbage collection instead of efficiently updating existing elements.
howToFix: Add a trackBy function to *ngFor that returns a unique stable identifier
For @for blocks, add a track expression using a unique property
Ensure the tracked property is stable across data refreshes
